lastUpdateSummary.headline: Windward says selected ships are now exiting Hormuz by hugging Iran’s coast, making the selective-passage regime more operationally visible.
lastUpdateSummary.body: The clearest genuinely new signal in this pass is Windward’s 17 March routing note that at least five eastbound bulk carriers left the Gulf on 15-16 March via Iranian territorial waters rather than the normal international navigation channel, and that nearly all of those ships had previously called at Imam Khomeini. Windward also says two LPG carriers passed in the prior 36 hours and that eight non-Iranian AIS-on ships were tracked through the strait on 16 March, almost double earlier-in-the-week levels, even while overall traffic was still down about 97%. That strengthens the dashboard’s working picture from simple scarcity to permission-based corridor management: selected cargoes are moving, but through politically filtered routing patterns rather than a normal reopening of Hormuz.

last24hSummary.headline: The latest 24-hour picture still shows a highly restricted Gulf system, but with clearer evidence of selective routing inside Iranian-controlled waters.
last24hSummary.body: Across the latest review, no source showed a broad commercial normalization of Hormuz traffic. Windward’s new 17 March analysis is the main operational change: it says at least five eastbound bulk carriers exited via Iranian territorial waters on 15-16 March, with most having prior calls at Imam Khomeini, and that two LPG carriers also got through in the prior 36 hours. UKMTO’s incidents page now says the 16 March Fujairah tanker event was re-classified as suspicious activity after investigators assessed the damage as falling debris from interceptions in the vicinity, so this pass did not identify a fresh confirmed vessel attack in the last 24 hours. Hormuz Strait Monitor’s homepage continues to describe the strait as restricted and near-paralyzed, with only a handful of ships in transit. The regional read remains that flows can move in selected cases, but the corridor is still politically gated, operationally abnormal, and shaped by workaround routing rather than open market access.

vesselAttacks24hSummary.headline: No confirmed fresh vessel attack was verified in this pass; UKMTO now reclassifies the 16 March Fujairah tanker incident as suspicious activity.
vesselAttacks24hSummary.body: This specialist attacks run started with UKMTO Recent Incidents and then checked Lloyd's List security coverage plus targeted web corroboration. The material change in this pass is on UKMTO itself: report #23 for the 16 March tanker event 23 nautical miles east of Fujairah now carries Update 001 saying investigations indicate the vessel was subjected to falling debris from interceptions in the vicinity and that UKMTO has re-classified the incident as Suspicious Activity. That means there is no newer confirmed in-scope vessel attack in the last 24 hours. The latest confirmed attack still in the dedicated timeline is therefore UKMTO attack report #22 dated 12 March, when a container ship 35 nautical miles north of Jebel Ali was struck by an unknown projectile causing a small fire onboard. Lloyd's List and trade-press rewrites reviewed in this pass mostly preserved the earlier Fujairah strike framing, but the dashboard's attacks artifact now follows the current UKMTO classification as authoritative.

vesselAttacksLatest.place: 5NM south of Al Basrah, Iraq TTW
vesselAttacksLatest.summary: UKMTO attack report #21: two tankers were struck by unknown projectiles, both suffered fires onboard, and both crews were evacuated safely. No environmental impact was reported at the time of the updates.

vesselAttacksLatest.place: 50NM northwest of Dubai, United Arab Emirates
vesselAttacksLatest.summary: UKMTO attack report #20: a bulk carrier reported being hit by an unknown projectile. Crew were safe and well, with no environmental impact reported.

vesselAttacksLatest.place: 11NM north of Oman in the Strait of Hormuz
vesselAttacksLatest.summary: UKMTO attack report #19: a cargo vessel was hit by an unknown projectile, causing a fire onboard. The vessel requested assistance, the crew evacuated, and later updates said some crew remained unlocated.

vesselAttacksLatest.place: 25NM northwest of Ras Al Khaimah, United Arab Emirates
vesselAttacksLatest.summary: UKMTO attack report #18: a container vessel sustained damage from a suspected but unknown projectile. All crew were reported safe and accounted for, and later updates said there was no environmental impact.

vesselAttacksLatest.place: 35NM north of Jebel Ali, United Arab Emirates
vesselAttacksLatest.summary: UKMTO attack report #22: a container ship was struck by an unknown projectile, causing a small fire onboard. Crew were reported safe and authorities were investigating.

vesselAttacksLatest.place: 23NM east of Fujairah, United Arab Emirates
vesselAttacksLatest.summary: UKMTO report #23 initially described a tanker at anchor struck by an unknown projectile with minor structural damage and no injuries, but Update 001 later said investigators assessed the damage as falling debris from interceptions in the vicinity and UKMTO re-classified the incident as suspicious activity.
vesselAttacksLatest.statusLabel: Reclassified by UKMTO as suspicious activity

previousDaySummary.headline: March 16 showed a politically managed trickle through Hormuz, not a return to normal traffic.
previousDaySummary.body: The day began with the operating picture still dominated by extreme scarcity of visible movement: Windward's 16 March update recorded only three outbound Hormuz crossings and no inbound transits on 15 March, including the sanctioned VLCC NORA after loading about 2.01 million barrels at Kharg for China. Broader route-capacity reporting still put effective Middle East exports at only about one-third of normal levels despite record use of alternative outlets, with roughly 350 tankers stranded, Saudi western-port exports reaching 5.9 million barrels per day on 9 March and Fujairah's crude bypass capacity still limited to roughly 1.5-1.8 million barrels per day. Later on 16 March, MarineTraffic and Kpler flagged the Aframax Karachi carrying Das crude as the first non-Iranian cargo tanker seen clearing Hormuz with AIS active since the crisis escalated, while CNBC reported Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent saying Washington is tolerating ongoing Iranian oil tanker departures and that India is still seeking clearance for 22 additional ships. The combined signal from the day was that some cargoes can move, but only through selective diplomatic or political permission layered on top of a system that remains far from commercially normalized across Hormuz, Kharg, Yanbu, Fujairah and the waiting fleet offshore.
